items.q: Are monopolies always bad for consumers?
items.correct: Standard theory says monopolies restrict output and raise prices, but possible benefits include economies of scale, R&D investment and dynamic efficiency.
items.misconception: Monopolies are illegal and always harmful.
items.random_wrong: A monopoly is when one country dominates world trade.
items.why: Monopolies aren't automatically illegal — natural monopolies in utilities can be efficient. The analysis requires weighing static inefficiency against potential dynamic gains. Students treat the question as already settled.

items.q: What does Income Elasticity of Demand (YED) tell us?
items.correct: The sign tells us the good type: positive YED = normal good (rises with income); negative YED = inferior good (falls with income). YED > 1 indicates a luxury; YED between 0 and 1 a necessity.
items.misconception: A negative YED means the demand calculation went wrong.
items.random_wrong: YED measures how the rich and poor differ in tastes.
items.why: The negative sign for inferior goods (like own-brand pasta or bus travel) is meaningful, not an error. Students often treat negative elasticities as mistakes, missing what the sign signals about good type.

items.q: What does the assumption 'ceteris paribus' mean in economic models?
items.correct: 'All other things equal' — when analysing the effect of one variable, all other variables are held constant. It is an analytical simplification, not a claim about reality.
items.misconception: Ceteris paribus means everything happens at the same time in real markets.
items.random_wrong: Ceteris paribus is a Latin name for the equilibrium price.
items.why: Real-world markets never hold all things equal — ceteris paribus is a thought-experiment device. Students treat it as a description of reality, missing why economists use it: to isolate cause and effect.

items.q: Quantitative easing involves the Bank of England?
items.correct: Buying government bonds to inject money
items.why: Correct answer: Buying government bonds to inject money. “Raising interest rates” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which body protects UK consumers from mis-selling by banks?
items.why: Correct answer: FCA. “HMRC” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: £10,000 at 5% compound interest for 2 years. Final balance?
items.why: The real figure is 11025. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
